coredata.dc:title: Novel battery power capability assessment for improved eVTOL aircraft landing

Barai, Anup
Niri, Mona Faraji
Wang, Zeyuan
Marco, James
coredata.dc:description: A key aspect in the deployment of electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) aircraft is the safety and performance capabilities of the batteries used. A component of the safety requirements is the need for reserve energy, which would only be utilised in case of emergencies. In the literature, it has been observed that the lower bound of the reserve region of battery energy should be limited to avoid the area where a precipitous drop in voltage would occur. Here, a method for defining the lower bound is proposed. This seeks to extend the amount of time the aircraft can cruise before landing can no longer be completed. A novel power capability testing procedure is used to measure the lowest state-of-charge (SoC) at which a constant power pulse can be completed. This differs from existing power capability tests that perform pulses at predetermined SoC points. The goal of the proposed method is to replicate the conditions of landing to understand the power capability performance at low SoC. The test is performed for a variety of environmental conditions and use cases, which includes temperature and power pulses, as well as two sets of differently aged cells. For the defined test conditions, the lowest SoC values for the calendar aged cells ranges from 6% to 14% SoC, whereas the range for cycle aged cells is 8% to 27% SoC. The outcome of this test is a characteristic map which correlates temperature, pulse power and pulse duration to the lowest SoC the pulse can be completed. The characteristic map indicates the lowest SoC value allowed for the battery before landing needs to be performed. The accuracy of the characteristic map is compared to a battery equivalent circuit model, parametrised from the test data. The defined approaches are experimentally validated based on a set of previously unmeasured experimental conditions. Overall, the characteristic map provides good accuracy compared to measured values, with both map and model methods having an average maximum absolute percentage error of at most 7.5%. Additionally, the test results show that if the worst case landing scenario is used as the lower bound of the reserve region, the useable SoC range for nominal flight would be greatly affected if cell degradation is not considered.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Experimental schematic. (b) Photographs of the cell setup used for power capability testing, including the cell test rig inside thermal chamber and cell setup within the test rig.

Flowchart showing the steps of the proposed power capability testing procedure. Power capability test stages 1 and 2 are performed for both of the pulses assigned to a cell, and all the defined temperatures.
Plot of pulse duration against pulse power for the conditions used for power capability testing and validation. The grey rectangle indicates the region covered by the characteristic map.
Diagram of the ECM used to model a single cell. The battery terminal voltage is outputted based on an inputted current value.

Open circuit voltage and internal resistance measurements from calendar (a,b) and cycle aged cells (c,d). (a) and (c) Mean OCV, mean OCV cell-to-cell difference with standard deviation error bars, and maximum OCV cell-to-cell difference from 16 cells. (b) and (d) Internal resistance measurements for 16 cells.

Example of current profile and voltage response of stage 1 of the proposed power capability testing methodology. (a) Current profile applied to four cells, for a 40 W 30 s power pulse. (b) Measured voltage from four cells when stage 1 of the power capability test is applied, with zoomed sections of the first, and second to last pulse performed.

Example of current profile and voltage response of stage 2 of the proposed power capability testing methodology. (a) Current profile applied to one cell, for a 40 W 30 s power pulse. (b) Measured voltage from 4 cells when stage 2 of the power capability test is applied, with a zoomed plot of a pulse, and a zoomed plot of the voltage after the SoC adjustment.

Example of cell surface temperature measurements, taken from stage 1 of the power capability testing methodology. The temperature is shown for one cell of each pulse condition used. The ambient temperature of the test was 25 °C.
Power capability characteristic maps of (a) calendar aged cells and (b) cycle aged cells.
Extracted model parameters for calendar and cycle aged cells, from power capability testing results stage 1. Parameters for cycle aged cells at 15 °C were extracted from power capability stage 2.
Summary of the factors for power capability testing with their corresponding values.

Results from power capability test validation for calendar aged cells. The measured results were determined for each pulse with four cells; the characteristic map results were interpolated from the characteristic map based on pulse duration, pulse power, and temperature were used as inputs; and the 2RC model refers to the lowest whole SoC a simulated pulse was completed. The estimation results were shown in terms of MAPE.
Results from power capability test validation for cycle aged cells. The same methodology for calculating the results from Table 2 was used here.

head.ce:title: Novel battery power capability assessment for improved eVTOL aircraft landing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A key aspect in the deployment of electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) aircraft is the safety and performance capabilities of the batteries used. A component of the safety requirements is the need for reserve energy, which would only be utilised in case of emergencies. In the literature, it has been observed that the lower bound of the reserve region of battery energy should be limited to avoid the area where a precipitous drop in voltage would occur. Here, a method for defining the lower bound is proposed. This seeks to extend the amount of time the aircraft can cruise before landing can no longer be completed. A novel power capability testing procedure is used to measure the lowest state-of-charge (SoC) at which a constant power pulse can be completed. This differs from existing power capability tests that perform pulses at predetermined SoC points. The goal of the proposed method is to replicate the conditions of landing to understand the power capability performance at low SoC. The test is performed for a variety of environmental conditions and use cases, which includes temperature and power pulses, as well as two sets of differently aged cells. For the defined test conditions, the lowest SoC values for the calendar aged cells ranges from 6% to 14% SoC, whereas the range for cycle aged cells is 8% to 27% SoC. The outcome of this test is a characteristic map which correlates temperature, pulse power and pulse duration to the lowest SoC the pulse can be completed. The characteristic map indicates the lowest SoC value allowed for the battery before landing needs to be performed. The accuracy of the characteristic map is compared to a battery equivalent circuit model, parametrised from the test data. The defined approaches are experimentally validated based on a set of previously unmeasured experimental conditions. Overall, the characteristic map provides good accuracy compared to measured values, with both map and model methods having an average maximum absolute percentage error of at most 7.5%. Additionally, the test results show that if the worst case landing scenario is used as the lower bound of the reserve region, the useable SoC range for nominal flight would be greatly affected if cell degradation is not considered.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Partnership for a new generation of vehicles.

ce:section.ce:para: Electric vertical take-off and landing (eVTOL) aircraft are a recent form of battery powered transport system. They are part of a goal to make zero emission urban air mobility (UAM) more feasible [1]. UAM can refer to inter- and intra-city air passenger transport using VTOL aircraft [2]. The operation of eVTOL and UAM are currently limited by infrastructure, which includes landing pads (referred to as vertiports) [3], air traffic management [2], and airspace design [1,3].

Typical eVTOL mission profiles can be summarised into three main parts, take-off, cruise, and landing [4,5]. To date, no openly available eVTOL load profile data sets are available, although examples of representative profiles can be found in many research papers. These show that the take-off and landing components of the profile require significantly more power than cruising [6]. More specifically, in [5], for take-off and landing, the average power to energy ratio is 3, whereas cruising have an average power to energy ratio close to 1. The usable state-of-charge (SoC) range for nominal flight is restricted by the requirement for reserve energy, which is only deployed in case of emergencies [6]. Additionally, in [7], a battery floor is defined as the very low SoC region (10% [8]) that should not be counted as battery reserves, due to precipitous voltage drops causing current spikes. This would define the lower bound of the reserve region, with the upper bound defined from the reserve requirements with respect to the lower bound.

For eVTOL aircraft, power capability is a critical performance metric to ensure the completion of landing [6]. This is especially true for emergencies, in which the battery may approach the discharge cut-off voltage. Therefore, power capability can be described as the primary end-of-discharge condition. It can also be regarded as a safety critical metric. So, when defining the lower bound of the reserve window, the power capability needs to be one of the primary factors considered. To date, methods used to determine the reserve region or battery floor have not been presented in the literature. Additionally, little research has been conducted assessing both the safety and performance requirements for the landing of eVTOL aircraft.

Power capability is defined as the maximum power that can be used to charge or discharge a battery, with respect to maximum and minimum voltage limits [9,10]. The power capability of a battery is dependent on its electrochemistry and form factor [11], with primary limitations being the electron conductivity [12] and ion diffusivity in the electrodes and electrolyte [13–15]. On a cell level, power capability is dependent on SoC [10], discharge duration [10,16], cell temperature [17,18], and the amount of power fade (i.e., degradation) [19]. More specifically, testing is performed in [20] to measure the relationship of power capability with temperature and SoC. It is shown when these value are lowered, power capability decreases. Further examples of this are shown in [17,21].

Power capability measurements have historically taken the form of pulse power testing. One such test is the hybrid pulse power characterisation (HPPC) test, which is introduced in the partnership for a new generation of vehicles (PNGV) battery test manual [22], in 1997. This test has been used to determine the dynamic power capability of a battery at 10% SoC increments, using constant current charge and discharge pulses. This test can also be used to derive the cell resistance and cell polarisation resistance as a function of SoC. Very little development in this testing methodology has occurred, with a more recent battery test manual from the Idaho National Laboratory [23], published in 2015, containing the same test procedure. Similar tests have been performed as part of research. These are defined based on the target application, which primarily focus on electric vehicles (EVs). Examples using of HPPC tests can be found in various publications. Many of these tests are performed in the same manner but consider different discharge pulses and SoC increments. Note that the pulse current is sometime considered as a C-rate, which is defined as the current relative to the nominal battery capacity. For example, in [24], the pulse testing is performed using 40 s pulses at 65% and 35% SoC, with C-rates of 3C, 5C, and 10C. In [25], pulses of lengths 0.1, 2, 5, 10, and 18 s and C-rates 1C, 2C, 5C, and 15C are used at 50% SoC to measure the resistance of a cell, which determines the power capability. In [10], multiple 5 and 10 s pulses are performed using different charge and discharge currents, at 10% SoC intervals. Furthermore, in [26,27], different power capability testing methodologies are reviewed. For the tests considered, the measured variable is either terminal voltage or discharge time, when a constant current pulse is applied to a cell.

For transport applications, the batteries usually fall somewhere on an ageing spectrum based on their usage history. There are two categories of ageing, namely cycle ageing and calendar ageing [28,29]. Cycle ageing refers to how degradation affects battery performance and characteristics through the application of repeated charge and discharge cycles [30,31]. Calendar ageing refers to the performance degradation as a result of the cell undergoing minimal use [19,32]. Pulse testing has been performed to measure the amount of power capability lost due to both cycle ageing [33,34] and calendar ageing [19,35]. This loss is referred to as power fade. Some studies determine power fade from the increase in internal resistance [35]. In real-world applications, cells will undergo a combination of these phenomena [28,36,37]. Although in [5], it is inferred that for eVTOL cycle ageing would have a more significant impact than calendar ageing.

Typically, cells for eVTOL would be freshest for new aircraft or if a recent battery swap has been performed. Cycle ageing would occur from the use of the aircraft [38,39]. Calendar ageing can occur due to the aircraft batteries are inactive [32], which could occur from supply chain issues, in which there is a large time period between the cells being manufactured and the cells being implemented for deployment. This can result in cells that are not always “fresh” when first used. Due to the high power requirements needed to perform landing, in [6], it is identified that a suitable end-of-life criteria would be power fade. Therefore, it is important to examine the power capability of cells in different ageing states, especially highly degraded cells. In addition, regardless of the ageing condition of the cell, the amount of power needed to land will remain the same. Therefore, a testing method is required to determine how much a cell can be discharged before the power capability falls below a certain value. For existing tests, it is difficult to gather information about the point when a power pulse can no longer be performed as they primarily use predetermined SoC breakpoints.

The motivation for this research is to develop a novel power capability testing framework. The purpose of this testing framework is to measure the lowest SoC at which a constant power pulse can be fully completed. Testing cells to their minimum discharge voltage limit will indicate the maximum amount the cell can be discharged before the required power for landing cannot be provided. This test is performed using various operating conditions, with the aim of replicating the conditions a cell may be subjected to under eVTOL landing (extended high power discharge at low SoC). An additional contribution of this research is the proposed tests can provide insight into the usable SoC range of batteries for eVTOL aircraft, which has not been thoroughly investigated in previous studies. More specifically, a battery floor has been described in the literature, which restricts the lower limit of the reserve energy. Hence, this testing can be used to better determine the minimum SoC of the battery floor. This has the advantage of lowering the SoC region of the reserve energy, thus increasing the overall amount of energy available for nominal flight. The primary outcome of these tests is to produce a characteristic map which correlates temperature, pulse power and pulse duration to the lowest SoC the pulse can be completed. A secondary outcome is to test the viability of the pulse data being used to characterise a battery model, so that a model-based power capability estimation method could be implemented.

The remainder of the paper is organised as follows: Section 2 describes the experimental testing approach, including the test setup and the test procedure. Section 3 covers the modelling approach used to estimate power capability. In Section 4, results for characterisation, model parametrisation, and power capability mapping are presented. This section also includes experimental results of the power capability estimation methods. In the final sections, Section 5 and Section 6, conclusions of the work and potential directions of future work are given.

In this research, the cells used for testing are commercial Samsung 48X cells. These cells are 21700 cylindrical cells, with a Lithium Manganese Nickel chemistry. The nominal voltage of these cells is 3.64 V, with a voltage operating range of 2.5 V to 4.2 V. The typical capacity is 4.8 Ah. For charging and discharging, the current values used here are shown in terms of C-rate, which is defined as the current used relative to the battery capacity. Therefore, for the cell used in this investigation, 1C is 4.8 A. For the testing performed, discharging is performed using constant current (CC) and charging is performed using constant current constant voltage (CCCV), with a cut-off current of C/20. The testing environment consists of 16 cells placed into Arbin cell holders, connected to a Digatron cycler, with a K-type thermocouple sensor placed centrally on the surface of each cell. The thermocouples have an accuracy of
± 0 . 1
°
C. The cells are controlled through the cycler, such that current or power values are the inputs, and the voltage and temperature are the measured outputs. These thermocouples are used for safety to ensure the cell does not go into thermal runaway by providing the host PC with real-time temperature data. For the testing performed, the temperature safety threshold is set to 65 °C. The cells are placed in a climate controlled Espec chamber, which allows for the temperature of the environment to be regulated. The setup used is shown in Fig. 1.

For the power capability study performed here, two different groups of cells are tested. The cells are categorised as cells aged through cycling and new cells. The new cells spent 18–24 months in storage and stored for unknown amount of time at unknown conditions after being manufactured and during transit. While in storage, the cells were placed into a storage container which had an ambient temperature of 15 °C. The cell’s SoC was left unchanged, meaning that they were stored with the shipping SoC, which was approximately 30% SoC. When the capacity of these cells were measured (using a C/3 discharge current), they had been degraded by 1.3% compared to the minimum capacity on the cell’s data sheet (4.7 Ah, measured with C/5 and 1C discharge currents). Therefore, in this paper, these cells are marked as calendar aged cells instead of new cells.

For the cycle aged cells, the ageing regime involved cycling strings of 8 parallel cells. This allowed for a closer replication of the cell ageing in a battery module. The number of parallel-connected cells indicate the capacity and maximum amperage of the energy storage system [40], so increasing the number of cells in parallel is a common method to increase the capacity. Parallel-connected cells have the same output voltage, meaning variation in cell characteristics can cause changes in the current distribution between cells [40]. The cycling regime used to age the cells included a C/3 CCCV charge, with a C/24 current cut-off; a five-minute rest; an eVTOL mission profile, performed until the end-of-discharge voltage was reached; and another five minute rest. This regime was implemented to replicate similar conditions batteries an eVTOL aircraft would experience. The eVTOL mission profile used was a bespoke design, which contained take-off, cruising, and landing sections. The profile is 2039 s long and had an average power of −101.9 W. The voltage response of the profile is shown in [41]. This mission profile was performed at 55 °C and repeated for 150 cycles. This temperature was used to replicate eVTOL battery pack temperatures during usage. The amount of degradation is determined through the capacity state-of-health (SoH) of the cells, calculated from C/3 CC discharge capacity measurements.

The proposed testing procedure is based on the concept that for eVTOL, power capability determines the end-of-cruise condition for both nominal flight and emergencies, where the reserve energy needs to be deployed. Therefore, in order to maximise the energy that can be extracted from the batteries, it is crucial to know how much a cell can be discharged before the power capability falls below a required value. This would indicate that landing needs to be performed. SoC is chosen to represent how much that a cell have been discharged, with the lowest SoC indicating the limit where power capability falls below the required amount. For this, power capability is determined by the power and the duration of a constant power pulse.

To obtain the lowest SoC at which a constant power pulse can be fully completed, an efficient two stage pulse testing procedure is designed. Stage 1 is performed by repeatedly discharging a cell with a pulse of a predetermined power and duration, until the voltage falls below 2.5 V, hence, fails to be completed. From this, an initial guess for lowest SoC that the pulse is completed can be obtained. This lowest SoC is then used as the initial SoC of stage 2, where the pulse is performed at distinct SoC intervals, to obtain a more accurate value. Using this data, the lowest SoC could be calculated, to the nearest whole SoC percentage.

The overall power capability testing methodology flowchart is shown in Fig. 2, outlining each test step that is performed. The first step is to set the chamber temperature and allow the cell to reach thermal equilibrium. For each temperature, a standard capacity test is performed at the beginning and end of the power capability test. This is used to restore some reversible capacity loss due to the extended time in storage. The data can also be compared to determine the capacity loss from the testing. The standard capacity involves three C/3 CCCV (C/20 cut-off) charge and CC discharge cycles. Next, each cell is assigned a power pulse. In order to quantify if any cell-to-cell power capability variation is present, 4 cells are tested with each pulse. Stages 1 and 2 of the power capability test are then performed. In stage 1, no SoC adjustment is used, and in stage 2, the SoC is adjusted so that the test is performed 1% SoC increments. This value can be changed to find the lowest SoC at a finer resolution. The minimum voltage is set to 2.5 V which is the discharge cut-off voltage in the cell’s datasheet. Using this limit enabled the maximum energy that could be discharge from a battery with a power capability limitation to be found.

To define the test conditions, the factors that affect power capability performance are found from the literature. These are discharge power, discharge duration, temperature, and level of degradation [16,42,43]. Usually, pulse power capability testing has been performed using constant current, such as in [16,22,24]. Here, constant power is used for two reasons. Firstly, for eVTOL applications, flight profiles have been defined in terms of power. Secondly, power discharge can provide greater insight into cell-to-cell variation, as voltage differences between the cells will change the amount of current applied to each cell. Furthermore, in [22], constant current pulses are used instead of constant power pulses in order to avoid voltage-current interactions and provide better test repeatability [44].

Control of the pulse power and duration is set through the test program design for the battery cycler. Temperature of the cell is set by adjusting the climate controls of the test chamber and allowing the cell’s temperature to stabilise. These parameters are easily quantifiable and controlled through the test setup. Controlling the degradation of a battery can be challenging due to the affects that different conditions may have on the performance parameters [45,46]. Battery degradation causes changes to capacity, internal resistance, and open circuit voltage (OCV) [36,47], which all can affect a cell’s power capability [33,34]. Furthermore, changes to these quantities are not always correlated [31,48], which makes power capability analysis a useful and important tool.

For the design of the test conditions, each factor is tested with multiple test points. The pulse power and duration values are designed to replicate eVTOL landing conditions, whilst not exceeding the manufacturer’s cell design limits. Due to the lack of data from eVTOL manufacturers in the public domain, examples of eVTOL drive cycles are found in the literature. Many of these exceed the safety limits of a single Samsung 48X cell, which is used here. For example, in [4], a 54 W 105 s pulse is used for landing, whereas the safety limits of the Samsung 48X only allow for a continuous discharge of 38 W. Nevertheless, the durations of the landing used here are determined from the literature. Short duration landings of 30 s are used in [49,50], whereas longer duration landings of 100 to 120 s are used in [4,51,52]. Due to discharge rate limitations of the cell used, a shorter duration is employed, so that a higher power could be used. Therefore, the pulse duration test points used are 30 s and 40 s. Consequently, the pulse power test points are chosen to be between 30 W and 45 W.

Power capability is known to have a large dependency on temperature [44,53], so, a number of temperature test points are considered. The temperature bounds are determined from analysis of the use case and cell performance. Since landing occurs at the end of the mission profile, the cell will undergo self-heating during the flight, resulting in a temperature greater than that before take-off. In [4], the end-of-cruise and end-of-landing temperature difference is 30 °C, and in [38], a temperature difference of 20 °C is obtained from simulation of an air-cooled battery pack. Therefore, the lower temperature bound is set at 15 °C to account self-heating if the ambient temperature is close to 0 °C. In order to keep the temperature within datasheet limits, the ambient temperature is limited such that the surface temperature of the cell does not exceed 60 °C. Therefore, the upper temperature bound is set at 40 °C.

To test how degradation affected the proposed power capability testing, two groups of aged cells are tested: calendar aged and cycle aged. The conditions of ageing are discussed previously in Section 2.1. Many examples can be found of ageing causing a decrease in power capability, such as in [19,33]. Here, the goal is not to analyse the root causes of degradation, but instead to understand their impact on power capability performance. To distinguish between different groups and to quantify how the degradation has affected cell performance, an OCV-HPPC characterisation test is applied to the cells to measure their internal resistance and OCV. The test consists of 10 s 2C pulses performed at 5% SoC intervals, with a 4 h rest period before successive pulses to ensure a reliable OCV measurement [54]. This test is applied to 16 cells of each age group to determine the amount of cell-to-cell variation in each group. For this, the SoC is calculated based on the ratio of remaining capacity and measured discharge capacity.
The test conditions used are shown in Table 1. Since there are 8 pulse power and duration combinations, the framework shown in Fig. 2 is performed twice, as each cell is assigned two pulses.

The outcome of this test is a power capability characteristic map for each ageing condition, where landing power, landing duration, and temperature are used as inputs to estimate the lowest SoC allowed for a successful landing of the aircraft. Characteristic maps have been commonly used to show the relationship between power capability and various control variables [55].

Here, MATLAB is used to process the data collected from the battery cycler. This includes calculating the lowest SoC value for each cell. The average lowest SoC value at which each pulse is performed is calculated for each test point. A three dimensional surface is created to represent the measured results, where the lowest SoC is correlated with the landing power and landing duration. This is repeated for all test temperatures. The measured data can be utilised to find unmeasured points by using interpolation. For this, the MATLAB function interp3 is used [56]. This function allows the input of pulse power, pulse duration, and temperature, in order to output an estimation of the lowest SoC at which this pulse could be fully completed.

For validating the characteristic maps, a set of pulse points are defined to test the accuracy of interpolating unmeasured values. The first point is chosen to be very close to an existing test point, so a 41 W 31 s pulse is used. The second and third points are chosen as edge cases of the map, so a 32.5 W 40 s pulse is used as that pulse duration is tested for but the pulse power is not, and a 45 W 35 s pulse is used as the pulse power is used but the duration is not. The final point chosen is a 37.5 W 35 s pulse, as this fell directly in the centre of the characteristic map. The validation tests are performed at three temperatures, 25 °C, as this temperature is used during the power capability testing, and 20 °C and 35 °C are used as they correspond to temperatures between the power capability surfaces measured.
The test points for the pulses used for testing and validation are shown in Fig. 3. The region the characteristic map fills is also indicated.

An additional goal of the proposed power capability testing is to verify how suitable a model-based approach is at replicating the results from the testing; in particular, modelling power pulses to determine the lowest SoC at which it can be fully completed. The disadvantages of characteristic map approaches often include large data storage requirements and the large numbers of tests required to map all possible use-case scenarios [57]. Additionally, these maps are based on steady-states, so they have an inability to consider the effects of battery non-linearity [55]. Hence, model-based methods have been previously implemented to improve power capability estimation [57,58]. Here, a second order equivalent circuit model (2RC ECM) is implemented [59,60]. This model is chosen for its computationally efficiency and voltage response accuracy, meaning it is suited for onboard real-time implementation. The model is parametrised and implemented using MATLAB.

/
( 3600 Q )
,
where
S o C
is the state-of-charge at a specific sample time, and Q is the measured capacity.

From the datasheet of the cells used, values for standard and rated capacity are given. The value for typical capacity is 4.8 Ah and the value for minimum capacity is 4.7 Ah. To determine the capacity-based SoH values for the cells used, the lower limit is chosen. For the calendar aged cells, the average SoH is 98.7%. This indicates that calendar ageing slightly reduces energy capacity of the cells. For the cycle aged cells, the average SoH is 89.4%. This value shows a significant amount of capacity fade was caused by cycling.

In order to quantify differences in performance characteristics between the cells used, the OCV and internal resistance measurements from different cells are compared. The results are shown in Fig. 5. For the OCV results, the maximum OCV difference is used to indicate the cell-to-cell variation in OCV. In Fig. 5(a), it is shown that between 100% and 20% SoC, the OCV difference is mostly between 2 mV and 4 mV, whereas in the low SoC region (less than 20% SoC), the difference rose rapidly to 15 mV. When comparing this to the mean difference, the increase in the low SoC region is not as large, but still a significant increase compared to higher SoC values. Additionally, the standard deviation error bars show there is little cell-to-cell variation between 20% and 100% SoC, but a large variation below 20% SoC. For the cycle aged cells, shown in Fig. 5(c), the OCV difference is large for the whole SoC range. The max OCV difference is also significantly larger than the mean OCV difference. This implies that cycle ageing can cause large OCV variations between cells. For the internal resistance, shown in Fig. 5(b) and (d), the majority of cells have the same values, with two cells being clear outliers for both groups of cells. Overall, the difference in internal resistance between cycle and calendar aged cells is large. For the results between 100% and 20% SoC, the cycle aged cell’s resistance is on average approximately 2 m Ω larger than the calendar aged cells. In terms of voltage response, this would cause a voltage decrease of 20 mV if a 10 A current is used, which would indicate that degradation can significantly affect the power capability. These results are consistent with previous studies reported in the literature [28,36].

Examples of power capability test stages 1 and 2 for the calendar aged cells are shown in Figs. 6 and 7, respectively. The measured current values from stage 1 are shown in Fig. 6(a), and the corresponding cell-to-cell variation in the terminal voltages is highlighted in Fig. 6(b). Pulses near the start and end of the test are emphasised. For the first pulse, two of the cells reach a lower voltage compared with the other two. The largest voltage difference at the end of pulse is 28 mV, between cells 2 and 3. This is driven by differences between the internal resistance and polarisation overpotential, since the OCV difference before and after the pulse is at most 1 mV. In the second to last pulse (the last fully completed pulse), higher cell-to-cell voltage differences are observed. The largest difference voltage is 154 mV, between cell 1 and cell 4. This is caused by multiple factors. Firstly, due to cell-to-cell variation in voltage, the cells will undergo different amounts of discharge currents, shown in Fig. 6(a). Over a long test period, the differences in capacity removed will accumulate, causing the cell’s SoC values to diverge. This divergence manifests as differences in OCV, which is shown by the large difference in the relaxed voltages between the cells. The largest difference in voltages before the pulse is measured as 26 mV. Secondly, this difference is also caused by either variation in the polarisation overpotential (possibly diffusion rate differences) or OCV variations. This can be seen from the voltage difference between cell 1 and cell 2 increasing over the length of the power pulse.

For stage 2, the cell-to-cell variations are highlighted in Fig. 7. Since the SoC is adjusted after each pulse, which can be seen between the high power pulses in Fig. 7(a), each cell is at the same SoC when the pulse is applied. Since the profile performed is dependent on the capacity, differences in this value cause the pulses to be performed at different points in time. During the first pulse shown in Fig. 7(b), the maximum voltage difference is measured as 46 mV between cell 2 and cell 3. When compared to the results from stage 1, this difference shows that larger cell-to-cell variation occurs at lower SoC values, even when the difference in relaxation voltages between cells is small. This also shows that the OCV of the four cells shown remains similar during the calendar ageing, as a maximum voltage difference of ∼ 8 mV during the relaxation stages. After stage 2 is completed, the lowest SoC value the pulse is completed and stored in a characteristic map. This test is completed for the defined power pulses and temperatures.

In addition to voltage and current measurements, temperature is also recorded. This is primarily for safety, but the measurements can be used to show the thermal responses of different cells. Fig. 8 shows temperature measurements from cell undergoing different power pulses. As expected, temperature during the pulses increase when higher power is used and when the cell is at lower SoC values. This is a result of the increase in current that is used on the cells. Also, the temperature measurements show the surface temperature returns to the initial value after the rest period following the power pulse.

The characteristic maps obtained from the power capability testing are shown in Fig. 9. The results from the testing show the expected outcome of the minimum SoC at which a pulse can be performed decreases when a shorter pulse duration or a smaller pulse power are used. Additionally, it is shown that the minimum SoC reduces when the temperature is increased. The error bars on each of the surfaces indicate the maximum and minimum values measured for the minimum SoC. This is used to express how the variation between the cells caused differences in the SoC results.

When comparing the characteristic maps for calendar aged cells, Fig. 9(a), and for cycle aged cells, Fig. 9(b), the effect of degradation can be seen through the increase in the minimum pulse SoC. This demonstrates that the usable SoC is reduced when high power capability is needed at low SoC. Additionally, the error bars for the cycle aged cells are much larger, reaching a magnitude of 5% SoC. For the calendar aged cells, the largest error bar is 1% SoC, meaning much greater cell-to-cell variation is present in the cycle aged cells. Additionally, power capability results for cycle aged cells show a greater dependence on temperature. This causes the surfaces to become more spread out, especially at higher pulse powers. This is the opposite of the observations for the calendar aged cells, which had similar minimum pulse SoC values for low pulses power, at 15 °C and 25 °C.

As well as defining a characteristic map, the pulse data is also used to define model parameters for a 2RC model. The parameter results for both sets of cells are shown in Fig. 10. These plots indicate how SoC, temperature, and ageing contribute to the battery model voltage output. Due to degradation, the cycle aged cells have a much smaller usable SoC window. Therefore, parameters are extracted from power capability testing stage 2 data for 15 °C to give a parameter values over a larger SoC range. Internal resistance is shown in Fig. 10(a), where a clear correlation between temperature and resistance is found. Furthermore, by inspecting the distances between the calendar and cycle aged cells, the relationship between temperature and
R
0
stays consistent through ageing. For the results shown, the first RC branch represents the fast dynamics, and the second RC branch represents the slow dynamics. For
R
1
and
τ
1
, shown in Fig. 10(b) and (c) respectively, the results show an increase in resistance values and a decrease in time-constant values at lower temperatures. This would translate to a more rapid rise and fall in the voltage overpotential of
U
1
than at higher temperatures. The results also suggest that for cycle aged cells, the overpotential from short time-scale dynamics increase at a significantly higher rate, compared with the calendar aged cells. This would result in worse power capability. For
R
2
and
τ
2
, shown in Fig. 10(d) and (e) respectively, the temperature and ageing dependence is not obvious. Overall, at low temperatures, the slow dynamics will cause greater overpotential, which is what is observed in the literature. For the ageing relationship, the results suggest that cycle ageing caused a small increase in
U
2
.

To validate the characteristic map and modelling approaches, a set of “unseen” pulse power points are performed at different temperatures. The results are shown in Tables 2 and 3. The measured (ground truth) lowest SoC is calculated from the mean value of four cells, so when a decimal is shown, i.e., 9.25% SoC, this indicates cells having different lowest SoC values. The characteristic map results for the SoC values are shown to one decimal place. As the measured results are found at 1% SoC intervals, these results could be rounded to the nearest whole SoC value. For the 2RC ECM, a profile of a power pulse is simulated from a relaxed state, based on the validation power pulses. The SoC is initialised at a whole SoC percentage value, and the lowest SoC is determined by the lowest SoC value used that the model voltage does not reach 2.5 V. The results of the characteristic map and the 2RC model are shown using maximum absolute percentage error (MAPE).

For the calendar aged cells, shown in Table 2, the characteristic map shows good accuracy with the measured results, while the 2RC model does not perform as well. At lower SoC values ( < 9%), the parameter data does not exist, and therefore, is spline extrapolated. This causes the lowest SoC values estimated to have a large inaccuracy in this region. For SoC values greater than 10%, the model accuracy is slightly more reliable. The accuracy in this region is within 10% of the measured values. This means there is potential for the fidelity of the modelling to be improved. Overall, the characteristic map shows much higher accuracy.

For the cycle aged cells, shown in Table 3, the characteristic map performs well again. For the 2RC model, the accuracy for 20 °C and 35 °C is close to the accuracy of the characteristic map, whereas at 25 °C, the model still has an error of 10% for many of the pulses. This is surprising as parameters for 20 °C and 35 °C are not measured, and thus are interpolated. Additionally, the measured SoC values show much more variation with temperature, compared with the calendar aged cells, implying that both map and model methods can provide reasonable accuracy, regardless of cell degradation. Once again, the characteristic map provides much better accuracy than the 2RC model.

The overall testing results show that for the landing profiles tested, a clear point where landing needs to be performed is found. Compared with existing testing methodologies that have been proposed, such as HPPC, the point where landing needs to be performed would be difficult to find and would most likely have to be estimated. When unknown factors need to be considered, such as landing profile and battery temperature, the worst case from the collected data would normally be used. For the results shown, this would correspond to the highest SoC value measured. This value would set the lowest SoC allowed before landing, which would inform the lower bound of the reserve energy region. For example, when calendar aged cells are used, the lowest SoC of the reserve region would be 14% SoC. Furthermore, knowledge of battery health is important as it has a significant on this value. For the cycle aged cells, the lower bound of the reserve region would be 27% SoC. Therefore, if battery health is not well tracked, the location of the reserve energy would have a considerable impact on the usable energy for nominal flight.

The safety requirements for eVTOL aircraft demand knowledge of the amount of power cells are able to supply. Here, a power capability testing methodology is proposed to replicate eVTOL landing by performing a high-power discharge pulse for an extended duration at low SoC. The novelty of this method is the ability to find the lowest SoC at which a power pulse can be fully completed, compared to existing methods that only consider the power capability at predefined SoC points. The resulting information can indicate the SoC region required for reserve flight, for which there has been a lack of exploration in the literature.

The outcome of the testing is a 3-dimensional characteristic map, which uses pulse power, pulse duration, and temperature as inputs to output the lowest SoC at which a constant power pulse can be fully completed. The data gathered is also used to characterise an ECM to compare a model-based approach to the characteristic map approach. Characteristic maps and battery models are generated for both calendar and cycle aged cells, which are validated against a set of unseen power pulses. Overall, interpolating the power pulses with the characteristic map produces more accurate results than the battery model, where the average maximum absolute percentage error of both methods is at most 7.5%. In addition, the proposed testing methodology provides greater insight into the usable SoC range for eVTOL. For worst case landing scenarios, the lowest SoC for the calendar aged cells is 14%, whereas the lowest SoC for cycle aged cells is 27%. This highlights the significant loss in power capability from cycle ageing, as well as the need for tracking the health of the batteries to increase performance and safety.

Possible future work can be split into two categories, experimental work and modelling work. For future experimental work, a comparative study with existing power capability can be performed to further show the effectiveness of the proposed method. Additional work would also include expanding the testing conditions for proposed power capability testing. Since the batteries used are high energy cells, testing a high power cell may be more suitable for high power testing at low SoC. Furthermore, high power cells would allow for higher power discharge to be used for longer durations. Expansion of the testing condition could also consider cell age, in particular, this testing could be implemented as a form of reference performance test to track the power fade as the cell is aged. The testing methodology could be expanded to find the lowest SoC a pulse could be completed at a greater SoC resolution, for example to the nearest 0.1% SoC. This would allow for greater accuracy and provide more insight into the cell-to-cell power capability variation but would require a longer testing duration.

Future modelling work would seek to improve the model accuracy in order to reliably reproduce the measured results. The characteristic map and battery model could also be used to develop state estimation algorithm appropriate for eVTOL applications. Since eVTOL aircraft perform a high power landing at the end of flight, their cut-off condition can be regarded as power capability, so an energy estimation algorithm which considers a power capability end-of-discharge condition could be implemented to improve remaining flight time estimation. This could be achieved by using future state prediction methods, such as ones used for remaining discharge energy and remaining discharge time estimation, to predict the point in time where landing can no longer be completed. These methods are implemented by the authors previously [41,66].
